---
title: "Cortisol Pathophysiology"
subtitle: "Unit 5, Part 3 — Cushing's Syndrome & Addison's Disease"
author: "Dr Mark Paul-Clark"
institute: "Nottingham Trent University"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default, ../styles-slides.scss]
    slide-number: true
    progress: true
    transition: fade
    chalkboard: true
    footer: "Unit 5: Cortisol | NTU"
---

## Harvey William Cushing (1869–1939)

- American neurosurgeon and endocrinologist
- Pioneer of brain surgery techniques
- First described the clinical syndrome of cortisol excess
- Linked pituitary basophil adenomas to the constellation of symptoms now bearing his name

---

## Cushing's Syndrome: Overview

**Definition:** Clinical syndrome resulting from **excessive exposure to glucocorticoids**

**Prevalence:** 10–15 million sufferers worldwide

### Three Major Causes

| Cause | Mechanism |
|-------|-----------|
| **Iatrogenic** | Prolonged exogenous GC treatment |
| **Adrenal tumour** | Autonomous cortisol secretion |
| **Excess CRH/ACTH** | Pituitary adenoma or ectopic ACTH |

::: {.callout-important}
**Fatal if untreated** — but treatable with adrenalectomy and glucocorticoid replacement therapy.
:::

---

## Three Major Endogenous Causes

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-width: 9
flowchart TD
    A[Cushing's Syndrome] --> B[Pituitary Adenoma<br/><i>Cushing's Disease</i>]
    A --> C[Adrenal Tumour]
    A --> D[Ectopic ACTH Syndrome]
    
    D --> E[Small cell lung carcinoma]
    D --> F[Bronchial carcinoid]
    D --> G[Thymic carcinoid/thymoma]
    D --> H[Pancreatic islet cell tumours]
    D --> I[Pheochromocytoma]
    D --> J[Medullary carcinoma]
    
    style A fill:#cc0000,color:#fff
    style B fill:#4a1486,color:#fff
    style C fill:#4a1486,color:#fff
    style D fill:#4a1486,color:#fff
```

::: {.callout-note}
**Cushing's disease** specifically refers to the pituitary adenoma cause; **Cushing's syndrome** is the broader term for any cause of cortisol excess.
:::

---

## Clinical Features of Cushing's Syndrome {.smaller}

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="33%"}
### Physical Signs
- Obesity — **90%**
- Hypertension — **85%**
- Plethora — 70%
- Hirsutism — 75%
- Striae — 50%
- Acne — 35%
- Easy bruising — 35%
- Moon face
- Buffalo hump
- Truncal obesity
- Thin extremities
:::

::: {.column width="33%"}
### Musculoskeletal
- Osteopenia — **80%**
- Proximal weakness — **85%**
- Muscle wasting

### Psychiatric (85%)
- Emotional lability
- Euphoria
- Depression
- Psychosis
:::

::: {.column width="33%"}
### Metabolic
- Glucose intolerance — 75%
- Diabetes — 20%
- Hyperlipidaemia — 70%
- Polyuria — 30%
- Kidney stones — 50%

### Immune
- **Increased susceptibility to infection**
:::

::::

---

## Addison's Disease: Overview

- **Thomas Addison** (1855) — first described adrenal insufficiency
- **Hypoproduction** of cortisol
- **Difficult to diagnose** clinically — insidious onset, non-specific symptoms
- Treated with **lifelong glucocorticoid replacement**

---

## Addison's Disease: Symptoms

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
### Chronic Symptoms
- Extreme fatigue
- Weight loss & decreased appetite
- **Hyperpigmentation** (primary only)
- Low blood pressure / fainting
- Salt craving
- Hypoglycaemia
- GI symptoms (nausea, vomiting, diarrhoea)
- Abdominal, muscle & joint pain
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
### Neuropsychiatric
- Irritability
- Depression
- Behavioural changes

### Other
- Body hair loss in women
- Sexual dysfunction

### Addisonian Crisis ⚠️
- Severe hypotension
- **Hyperkalaemia**
- Life-threatening emergency
:::

::::

---

## Primary vs Secondary Adrenal Insufficiency

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
### Primary (Addison's)
*Adrenal gland destroyed*

- Autoimmune adrenalitis (commonest in UK)
- Tuberculosis
- Other infections
- Metastatic cancer
- Adrenal haemorrhage

**→ Hyperpigmentation PRESENT**
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
### Secondary
*Pituitary/hypothalamic failure*

- Benign pituitary tumours
- Pituitary inflammation
- Prior pituitary surgery
- **Inappropriate corticosteroid withdrawal**

**→ NO hyperpigmentation**
:::

::::

---

## Why Hyperpigmentation in Primary Disease?

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-width: 9
flowchart LR
    A[Adrenal Failure<br/>↓ Cortisol] --> B[Loss of Negative<br/>Feedback]
    B --> C[↑ POMC Processing<br/>in Pituitary]
    C --> D[↑ ACTH]
    C --> E[↑ γ-Lipotropin]
    C --> F[↑ β-Endorphin]
    D --> G[Melanocortin<br/>Receptors]
    G --> H[↑ Melanin<br/>Deposition]
    
    style A fill:#cc0000,color:#fff
    style H fill:#8B4513,color:#fff
```

- In primary disease, **ACTH is elevated** because the adrenal cannot respond
- ACTH is co-derived from **POMC** (pro-opiomelanocortin)
- ACTH and related peptides activate **melanocortin receptors** → melanin deposition
- Visible in skin creases, buccal mucosa, scars
- In secondary disease, ACTH is **low** → no hyperpigmentation

---

## Comparing Cushing's and Addison's

| Feature | Cushing's Syndrome | Addison's Disease |
|---------|-------------------|-------------------|
| Cortisol | **↑↑ Excess** | **↓↓ Deficient** |
| ACTH | Varies by cause | ↑ (primary) / ↓ (secondary) |
| Weight | Obesity (central) | Weight loss |
| Blood pressure | Hypertension | Hypotension |
| Blood glucose | Hyperglycaemia | Hypoglycaemia |
| Skin | Striae, bruising, acne | Hyperpigmentation (primary) |
| Potassium | Low-normal | **Hyperkalaemia** |
| Immune | Immunosuppressed | Normal |
| Mood | Euphoria → psychosis | Depression, irritability |

---

## Summary: Part 3

:::{.incremental}
- **Cushing's syndrome** results from glucocorticoid excess — iatrogenic, adrenal, or ectopic ACTH
- Clinical features reflect the exaggerated physiological actions of cortisol across all systems
- **Addison's disease** results from cortisol deficiency — primary (adrenal) or secondary (pituitary)
- Hyperpigmentation distinguishes primary from secondary disease via the **POMC/melanocortin pathway**
- **Addisonian crisis** (hypotension + hyperkalaemia) is a medical emergency
- Both conditions are treatable but require lifelong management
:::

---

## Self-Assessment

<details>
<summary>**Q1:** A patient presents with cortisol excess, elevated ACTH, and no pituitary abnormality on MRI. What is the most likely diagnosis?</summary>
**Ectopic ACTH syndrome** — a non-pituitary tumour (e.g., small cell lung carcinoma, bronchial carcinoid) is producing ACTH autonomously. The elevated ACTH drives adrenal cortisol production, but the source is outside the pituitary.
</details>

<details>
<summary>**Q2:** Why does secondary adrenal insufficiency NOT cause hyperpigmentation?</summary>
In secondary insufficiency, the problem is at the pituitary or hypothalamus, so **ACTH production is reduced**, not elevated. Since hyperpigmentation is caused by ACTH-related peptides activating melanocortin receptors, low ACTH means no excess melanin deposition.
</details>

<details>
<summary>**Q3:** Explain the mechanism behind hyperkalaemia in Addison's disease.</summary>
The adrenal cortex also produces **aldosterone** (zona glomerulosa). In primary adrenal insufficiency, aldosterone production is impaired alongside cortisol. Aldosterone normally promotes renal potassium excretion and sodium retention. Its absence leads to **potassium retention (hyperkalaemia)** and sodium/water loss (hypotension).
</details>
